ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried bee hoon with fried chicken wings; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002700) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXA (stir-fried noodles) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for fried bee hoon with chicken wings exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Singaporean composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried bee hoon with fried chicken wings, approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of stir-fried rice vermicelli and two to three fried chicken wings.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon (HPB Singapore Foodo) and Fried Chicken Wing (HPB Singapore Foodo) — composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by combining HPB Foodo data for 'Fried Bee Hoon' (plain, per 100 g) and 'Fried Chicken Wing' (per 100 g) in approximate proportions reflecting a typical plate (60% bee hoon component, 40% fried chicken wing component). HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli / rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried bee hoon with fried chicken wings as a composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated using the rice vermicelli / rice noodle GI value (approximately 53, low GI) from the University of Sydney GI Database as the dominant carbohydrate component. The fried chicken wings contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 78 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.3, rounded to 40.5 accounting for dietary fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per plate)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the rice vermicelli contributes a moderate glycemic load (~40 per plate); pairing with non-starchy vegetables and reducing portion size can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from deep-fried chicken wings, and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its combination of high sodium (~1680 mg per plate), saturated fat from deep-fried chicken skin, and dietary cholesterol, all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Bee Hoon with Fried Chicken Wings is a popular Singaporean hawker and zi char dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (bee hoon) served alongside deep-fried chicken wings. The bee hoon component is wok-fried with garlic, eggs, bean sprouts, spring onions, and soy sauce, producing a savoury, slightly smoky flavour with a tender yet slightly chewy noodle texture. The fried chicken wings are marinated, coated, and deep-fried to achieve a crispy golden skin with juicy meat inside. This dish is a common comfort food in Singapore, widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and zi char stalls. Nutritionally, a standard plate (~400 g) provides approximately 780 kcal, with a moderate carbohydrate load from the rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low), significant protein from the chicken wings (~39 g per plate), and notable fat from the deep-frying process (~37 g per plate). Sodium content is high (~1680 mg per plate) due to soy sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is generally halal-friendly when prepared with permissible ingredients. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken and egg content.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo entries for fried bee hoon and fried chicken wing separately, as no single HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this exact combined dish. The GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact dish as served (with specific restaurant proportions) was not directly measured.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for a typical Singaporean fried bee hoon with fried chicken wings dish based on HPB Foodo component data. The GI of 53 is an analogous estimate using rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated based on 400 g serving size. The dish is sourced from field survey data (Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt - Circular Road), which may represent a Vietnamese-influenced variant; nutrient values reflect the standard Singaporean hawker preparation. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from typical frying oil composition and are not directly reported in HPB Foodo.
